summary: Austrian-born British philosopher of language, one of the most influential figures in analytic philosophy. Author of Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus (picture theory, 'whereof one cannot speak') and Philosophical Investigations (language games, private language argument, beetle in a box). His 'early' and 'late' periods represent a radical break in his thinking.

works.name: Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus
works.description: Wittgenstein's first book (1921), the only one published in his lifetime. It consists of seven numbered propositions expanding into sub-statements. Proposition 1: 'The world is everything that is the case.' Proposition 7: 'Whereof one cannot speak, thereof one must be silent.' The work introduces the picture theory of meaning (propositions picture facts in the world), analyzes truth-functions via NOR operations (Sheffer stroke), and compares itself to a ladder that must be discarded after climbing. Published with a Latin title in homage to Spinoza. Bertrand Russell's logical atomism drew on it. Frank Ramsey worked with C.K. Ogden on the first English translation and convinced Wittgenstein to return to Cambridge. It heavily influenced the Vienna Circle and logical positivism.

works.name: Philosophical Investigations & Language Games
works.description: Wittgenstein's posthumously published masterwork, representing his 'late' philosophy. It reverses many positions from the Tractatus, arguing that meaning arises from use in context ('meaning as use') rather than from picturing facts. Language games are illustrative examples of words in action: a builder and assistant communicating with 'block,' 'slab,' 'pillar,' 'beam.' Things we call 'games' share 'family resemblances' — overlapping traits with no necessary or sufficient condition. The book opens by criticizing a passage from Augustine's Confessions about naming objects, using the 'five red apples' shopkeeper example.

examples: a builder and an assistant communicate with words like 'block' and 'pillar' in one of this book's language games

clues.clue: family resemblances — overlapping traits, no necessary/sufficient condition for 'games'
examples: things we call 'games' have this semantic relationship, in which some entities share overlapping traits

examples: this book's opening describes a shopkeeper's responses to a slip reading 'five red apples' while criticizing a quote about naming objects from Augustine's Confessions

clues.clue: meaning as use — reverses picture theory; words have meaning through use in context
examples: the meaning of words can best be understood through their use in a given language game

examples: a late section of this book discusses a man with 'aspect-blindness' as analogous to someone lacking a 'musical ear'

examples: a claim in this book about philosophy having methods 'like therapies' inspired the therapeutic approach

works.name: Private Language Argument & Beetle in a Box
works.description: Wittgenstein's argument in Philosophical Investigations that a language understood by only one person is impossible because meaning requires shared standards of correctness. The beetle-in-a-box thought experiment imagines people each with a box containing something called a 'beetle' — since no one can look in anyone else's box, the word 'beetle' cannot refer to a private experience. The 'S' diary example involves someone writing 'S' each time they experience a particular sensation, but there is no standard of correctness. A.J. Ayer criticized the argument for failing to account for 'primary recognition.'

clues.clue: beetle-in-a-box — argues private language is impossible; no one can look in another's box
examples: this book rejects private languages with the beetle-in-a-box thought experiment
this philosopher used the example of a community of people who each have a box with a beetle inside

clues.clue: 'S' diary example — writing 'S' for a sensation with no standard of correctness
examples: includes a passage imagining someone who writes the letter 'S' in a diary each time they experience a particular sensation

clues.clue: A.J. Ayer criticized the argument for failing to account for 'primary recognition'
examples: A.J. Ayer criticized this argument because it fails to account for acts of 'primary recognition'

works.name: Rule-Following Paradox & Kripke's Interpretation
works.description: Wittgenstein's rule-following paradox holds that 'no course of action could be determined by a rule, because any course of action can be made out to accord with the rule.' Saul Kripke developed this in Wittgenstein on Rules and Private Language, introducing the 'quus' function — an operation that behaves like addition except when either summand exceeds 57, it returns 5. The 'Kripkenstein' portmanteau combines their names. James Conant argued these are 'Kantian' problems about conditions of possibility, not 'Cartesian' epistemological problems.

clues.clue: 'quus' function — addition-like but returns 5 for sums over 57 (Saul Kripke)
examples: an operation that ceases to be addition when either summand hits 57, called the 'quus' function
Kripke demonstrated the rule-following paradox with this absurd addition-like function

clues.clue: Kripkenstein — portmanteau of Kripke and Wittgenstein
examples: the names of these two philosophers are often merged into a monster-like portmanteau

clues.clue: schoolchild writing numbers on a blackboard — no guarantee of correct rule-following
examples: a thinker claimed that no amount of additional information can guarantee that another person is really doing this correctly, using the example of a schoolchild writing numbers

clues.clue: Kripke's book: Wittgenstein on Rules and Private Language
examples: a book by one of them titled for the other 'on Rules and Private Language'

works.name: On Certainty & Other Late Works
works.description: On Certainty responds to G.E. Moore's 'here is one hand' argument, questioning what it truly means to 'know.' Remarks on the Foundations of Mathematics discusses mathematical contradictions (Alan Turing argued 'the real harm will not come unless there is an application, in which a bridge may fall down'). Remarks on Colour analyzed Goethe and Runge to clarify language regarding color. Some Remarks on Logical Form was Wittgenstein's only academic paper.

clues.clue: On Certainty — responds to Moore's 'here is one hand'; questions what 'knowing' means
examples: one of this writer's last books opens by examining G.E. Moore's statement 'If you do know that here is one hand'

clues.clue: Turing debate — 'the real harm will not come unless... a bridge may fall down'
examples: this philosopher's acceptance of mathematical contradictions led an opponent to note that 'the real harm' comes when 'a bridge may fall down'

clues.clue: Remarks on Colour — analyzed Goethe and Runge
examples: one of this philosopher's later works analyzed Goethe and Runge in order to clarify language regarding color

clues.clue: Some Remarks on Logical Form — only academic paper
examples: this philosopher's only academic paper, 'Some Remarks on Logical Form'

works.name: General / Biographical & Cultural Presence
works.description: Wittgenstein studied under Bertrand Russell at Cambridge and came from a wealthy Viennese family. His brother Paul was a pianist who lost his right arm in WWI, inspiring Ravel's Piano Concerto for the Left Hand. Wittgenstein designed a house in Vienna (Haus Wittgenstein/Stonborough House) with Paul Engelmann in a style influenced by Adolf Loos. Thomas Bernhard wrote novels about the Wittgenstein family (Correction, Wittgenstein's Nephew). David Foster Wallace's The Broom of the System incorporates Wittgenstein's philosophy. David Markson's Wittgenstein's Mistress is about the last woman on Earth. Derek Jarman made a film about him. He loved watching movies (especially Tom Mix westerns). He was a patron of Georg Trakl. Jean-Francois Lyotard's concept of metanarratives was inspired by Wittgenstein.

clues.clue: asked whether thinking about 'logic or sins' — replied 'both' (by Russell)
examples: while pacing the floor, this philosopher was asked whether he was 'thinking about logic or his sins,' to which he replied 'both'

comprehensive_summary:
Ludwig Wittgenstein is one of the most frequently clued philosophers in quizbowl, with his two major works representing radically different philosophical positions. The Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus (1921), his only book published in his lifetime, consists of seven numbered propositions. Proposition 1 states 'The world is everything that is the case,' and Proposition 7 states 'Whereof one cannot speak, thereof one must be silent.' The work introduces the picture theory of meaning, uses NOR operations to define truth-functions, and compares itself to a ladder that must be discarded after climbing. It influenced logical positivism and the Vienna Circle. Frank Ramsey translated it into English with C.K. Ogden.

Philosophical Investigations, published posthumously, reverses the Tractatus's positions. It argues that meaning arises from use in context, not from picturing facts. Language games — such as a builder and assistant communicating with 'block,' 'slab,' 'pillar,' 'beam' — illustrate how words function in practice. Things we call 'games' share 'family resemblances' with no essential common trait. The book opens by criticizing Augustine's Confessions about naming objects.

The private language argument holds that a language understood by only one person is impossible. The beetle-in-a-box thought experiment shows that private experiences cannot be the meaning of words. The rule-following paradox, developed by Saul Kripke into the 'quus' function (addition that returns 5 for sums over 57), shows that no rule can determine its own application. On Certainty responds to G.E. Moore's 'here is one hand' argument.

Wittgenstein came from a wealthy Viennese family. His brother Paul was a pianist who lost his right arm in WWI, inspiring Ravel's Piano Concerto for the Left Hand. Wittgenstein designed Haus Wittgenstein with Paul Engelmann. Thomas Bernhard wrote novels about the family (Correction, Wittgenstein's Nephew). David Markson's Wittgenstein's Mistress and David Foster Wallace's The Broom of the System engage with his philosophy.

cards.front: Work: consists of seven numbered propositions, beginning with 'The world is everything that is the case' and ending with 'Whereof one cannot speak, thereof one must be silent'
Work: introduces the picture theory of meaning and compares itself to a ladder that must be discarded after climbing
Work: opens by criticizing a passage from Augustine's Confessions about naming objects, using a shopkeeper and a slip reading 'five red apples'
Concept: illustrative examples of words in action — a builder shouts 'block,' 'slab,' 'pillar,' 'beam' to an assistant; linked by 'family resemblances'
Concept: people each have a box containing something called a 'beetle,' but no one can look in another's box — argues that private experiences cannot be the meaning of words
cards.work: Private Language Argument & Beetle in a Box

cards.front: Concept: an addition-like function that returns 5 whenever either summand exceeds 57, introduced by Saul Kripke to illustrate a paradox about following rules
Work: responds to G.E. Moore's 'here is one hand' argument by questioning what it truly means to 'know' something

Philosopher: Alan Turing argued 'the real harm will not come unless there is an application, in which a bridge may fall down' in response to this philosopher's acceptance of mathematical contradictions
Concept: the semantic relationship in which entities defined by a term share overlapping traits but no necessary or sufficient trait obtains in all of them — used to describe 'games'
Philosopher: designed a house in Vienna with Paul Engelmann influenced by Adolf Loos, insisting on raising the ceiling 30 millimeters and spending a year on door handles
Philosopher: Thomas Bernhard's novel Correction follows a character who builds his sister a house called 'the Cone,' paralleling this philosopher's life
Philosopher: Frank Ramsey translated this thinker's first book into English with C.K. Ogden and convinced him to return to Cambridge
